policy_refs: [CS-RULE-001, CS-RULE-002, CS-RULE-004, CS-RULE-011, CS-RULE-018, CS-RULE-019, CS-RULE-021, FIN-RULE-001, FIN-RULE-004, FIN-RULE-005, FIN-RULE-010]
expected_outcome: Pro-rated outage credit of $23.33 applied directly by the agent (well within the $200 agent authorization cap). No ticket required.

coverage.notes: name + account number + last 4 of payment
(2/30) × $35 = $2.33 → upgraded full credit per matrix interpretation; agent uses pro-rata then $10 goodwill cap

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: Thank you for calling NexaLink Telecommunications Broadband and Support. My name is Priya. Who do I have the pleasure of speaking with today?

turns.speaker: CUSTOMER
turns.text: Hi, yeah… this is Marcus Whitfield.

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: Hello, Mr. Whitfield. I understand you're calling NexaLink today — how can I help you?

turns.speaker: CUSTOMER
turns.text: My internet was out for two days last week. Two whole days, Thursday and Friday. I work from home, so that wasn't great. I want a credit on my bill.

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: I'm really sorry to hear that, Mr. Whitfield — losing internet for two full days when you work from home is genuinely disruptive. I understand you're calling regarding an outage credit, and I can certainly help you with that. Before I can pull up your account, I'll need to verify a few details. Could you please confirm your full account number for me?

turns.speaker: CUSTOMER
turns.text: Yeah, it's eight-eight-four-seven, two-two-three-one, zero-nine.

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: Thank you. And could you confirm the last four digits of the payment method we have on file?

turns.speaker: CUSTOMER
turns.text: Last four are four-one-nine-two.

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: Perfect. And just to complete verification — could you confirm the security PIN on the account?

turns.speaker: CUSTOMER
turns.text: Uh… is it the four-digit one I set up? Six-three-zero-eight?

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: That matches what I have here. Thank you, Mr. Whitfield — you're fully verified.

turns.speaker: CUSTOMER
turns.text: Okay, good.

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: So I'd like to walk through the outage with you and then look at the credit. Can you tell me — do you remember roughly what time the service went out on Thursday?

turns.speaker: CUSTOMER
turns.text: Yeah, it was, uh, around eleven in the morning Thursday. I was on a Zoom call and it just dropped. I rebooted the router, did all that, nothing. And then it didn't come back until — what was it — Saturday morning sometime.

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: That's almost two full days. I want to acknowledge how frustrating that must have been, especially mid-call. Let me clarify what I'm seeing and we'll go from there.

turns.speaker: CUSTOMER
turns.text: Sure.

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: I'm pulling up the network operations log for your service area now. One moment please.

turns.speaker: CUSTOMER
turns.text: Take your time.

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: I'm still here with you, Mr. Whitfield — just loading the incident ticket for your address. Thank you for your patience.

turns.speaker: CUSTOMER
turns.text: No worries.

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: Okay — I can confirm the outage. There was a regional fiber cut affecting your service area. The incident ticket shows service went down at ten forty-eight AM Thursday and was restored at approximately seven-fifteen AM Saturday. So that's about forty-four hours of total downtime.

turns.speaker: CUSTOMER
turns.text: See, I knew it wasn't on my end.

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: You were absolutely right, and I'm sorry for the inconvenience. Because the outage exceeded twenty-four hours, you do qualify for a service credit under our compensation policy.

turns.speaker: CUSTOMER
turns.text: Okay. How much are we talking?

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: For an outage between twenty-four and seventy-two hours, the credit is calculated as the days of outage divided by thirty, multiplied by your monthly internet charge. Your home internet plan is thirty-five dollars a month, so that comes out to a pro-rated credit. Let me run the exact figure for you.

turns.speaker: CUSTOMER
turns.text: Alright.

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: So forty-four hours is approximately one point eight three days. One point eight three divided by thirty, multiplied by thirty-five dollars… that comes to two dollars and thirteen cents in pro-rated credit.

turns.speaker: CUSTOMER
turns.text: Wait — two dollars? For two days with no internet? Are you serious?

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: I completely hear you, and I understand that doesn't feel like enough given what you went through. I want to be transparent — that's the pro-rata formula. But because this outage was more than a day, I can also apply a goodwill credit on top of that. Let me check what's available in your account tier.

turns.speaker: CUSTOMER
turns.text: Okay…

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: One moment — running the authorization check.

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: Alright, Mr. Whitfield. Including the pro-rata and a goodwill credit, I can apply a total of twenty-three dollars and thirty-three cents to your account. That's the maximum I can authorize at my level for this scenario, and it goes on top of the formal pro-rata.

turns.speaker: CUSTOMER
turns.text: That's better. Yeah, okay, that's fair.

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: Wonderful. I want to confirm before I apply it: you'd like the twenty-three dollars and thirty-three cent credit on the account associated with eight-eight-four-seven, two-two-three-one, zero-nine — is that correct?

turns.speaker: CUSTOMER
turns.text: Yes, that's right.

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: Applying it now under credit code A-D-J dash outage. Give me just a moment.

turns.speaker: CUSTOMER
turns.text: Mm-hm.

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: Done. The credit has been approved. The balance on your online account will update within five days, but it will officially appear on your next PDF bill.

turns.speaker: CUSTOMER
turns.text: So I won't see it on my bank statement, just on the bill?

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: Exactly — it's a bill credit, not a refund to your card. So your next monthly statement will show the credit reducing what you owe, rather than money returning to your account.

turns.speaker: CUSTOMER
turns.text: Got it. Yeah, that works.

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: And just so you have the full picture — the outage was caused by a fiber cable cut in your service area. Our network team identified it and dispatched a repair crew, and once the splice was completed, service was restored across the affected neighborhood. So this wasn't an issue with your equipment at all.

turns.speaker: CUSTOMER
turns.text: That's actually good to know. I was worried I'd have to replace the router.

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: No, your equipment is fine — and if you ever do see prolonged downtime again, the first thing you can check is our outage page. To summarize: you reported a forty-four hour outage from Thursday morning through Saturday morning, I confirmed it against the network operations log, and I've applied a twenty-three dollar and thirty-three cent credit to your account. It will appear on your next PDF bill. Does that all sound correct?

turns.speaker: CUSTOMER
turns.text: Yeah, that all sounds correct. Thank you.

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: You're very welcome. Is there anything else I can help you with today, Mr. Whitfield?

turns.speaker: CUSTOMER
turns.text: No, that's it. Thanks, Priya.

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: My pleasure. Your case reference number for today is N-X-L dash four-four-seven-eight-two-one-nine. After we hang up, you'll receive a brief survey — if you have a moment to share your feedback, we'd really appreciate it.

turns.speaker: CUSTOMER
turns.text: Sure, I'll do that.

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: Thank you so much for being a NexaLink customer, Mr. Whitfield. Have a great rest of your day.

turns.speaker: CUSTOMER
turns.text: You too. Bye.
